data.description:
### 2. Incident Overview

* **Scenario Type:** Sub-Agent Blocker

#### Expected Behavior

test-create-dso-app.sh completes in under 10 seconds and leaves sufficient time budget for the next tests in the batch.

#### Actual Behavior

test-create-dso-app.sh takes ~42 seconds (measured: 42.047s wall time). The test-batched.sh default timeout is 50 seconds. When test-create-dso-app.sh is queued, it consumes nearly the entire time budget, after which test-batched.sh emits ACTION REQUIRED and exits. The next iteration starts fresh and queues test-create-dso-app.sh again since it was the last test completed before the batch ended. This causes validate.sh to remain PENDING across all iterations.

Measured: 59 assertions, 0 failures. The slowness is not due to failures — the test itself is slow by design.

#### Impact

validate.sh tests: gate cannot progress past tests/scripts. CI is unaffected (test-create-dso-app.sh is not in CI scope for tests/scripts).

#### Proposed Resolution Options

1. **Split the test into an integration test** — move to tests/integration/ and exclude from validate.sh default scope if the slowness is due to real filesystem or subprocess operations (e.g., cloning repos, running npm install).
2. **Optimize the test** — identify the slow operations (likely repo-template operations or subprocess spawning) and replace with stubs/fixtures.
3. **Register as slow test** — add a skip annotation or separate filtering so test-batched.sh can run it with a longer per-test budget and keep it out of the default batch ordering.
data.title: [test-create-dso-app.sh]: single test consumes ~42s -> exceeds 50s test-batched.sh budget, blocking batch progression
